data.title: Why We Are Optimistic About The Long-Term Outlook For Health Care
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Baron Health Care Fund Q2 2025 Shareholder Letter.

Health Care’s stretch of recent underperformance versus the broader equity markets reached unprecedented levels in the second quarter. The Benchmark underperformed the Index by over 17%! There are many reasons for the Health Care sector’s dramatic underperformance. Big picture, Health Care is a highly regulated industry which depends in significant part on government reimbursement, and the current Administration has proposed spending cuts and major policy changes that cause uncertainty around profits for many different Health Care sub-industries.

For example, in February, new leadership at the NIH, the government agency with a $50 billion annual budget which provides research grants to universities, announced a change in policy regarding the funding of indirect costs of research projects. While currently the subject of litigation, if the policy is implemented, some of the nation’s leading research universities each stand to lose hundreds of millions of dollars in research grants. In the meantime, the NIH has terminated or delayed research grants and the Trump Administration has proposed a 40% cut to the NIH budget for fiscal year 2026. Ultimately Congress will decide what next year’s NIH budget will be, but all of this uncertainty and disruption is causing headwinds for life sciences tools companies that sell products and services to academic laboratories.

For pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies, we await details around the Trump Administration’s plans for implementing a Most-Favored-Nation (MFN) drug pricing policy and for pharmaceutical sector tariffs. It’s unclear whether these policy proposals would withstand legal challenges, but the threat of these potential policies has raised questions about the potential impact on future earnings and R&D budgets. There have also been personnel changes and headcount reductions at the Food and Drug Administration (FDA), which have caused investor concerns that new drug approvals will be delayed or that the agency will change its criteria for approving drugs. Beyond these policy issues, Chinese biotechnology companies have emerged as a competitive threat and large multinational pharmaceutical companies have been partnering with Chinese biotechnology companies (where prices for early-stage companies are lower) at the expense of early-stage U.S. biotechnology companies. As a result, investors have been reluctant to deploy capital in the biotechnology sector. Although biotechnology funding improved in the June quarter versus the March quarter, biotechnology funding is still down year-over-year due to a very weak start to the year.

Outside of life sciences, managed care companies have suffered earnings shortfalls this year due to elevated medical cost trends in their Medicare and Medicaid books of business and the impact of changes to the risk adjustment rules for Medicare Advantage plans. Further, on July 4, President Trump signed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (the OBBBA) into law. The OBBBA includes more than $1 trillion in health care spending cuts over 10 years, mostly from Medicaid. This represents the biggest cut to federal health care spending and to Medicaid in history. Medicaid work requirements are scheduled to begin on January 1, 2027, and would require individuals to spend at least 80 hours each month working, completing community service, participating in a work program, or enrolling in an educational program. The Congressional Budget Office (CBO) has estimated that the work requirements would increase the number of uninsured people by 4.8 million by 2034, while other Medicaid provisions (immigration rules, more frequent eligibility checks) would add another 3 million to the uninsured number. In addition, the OBBBA curtails well-established state Medicaid funding mechanisms, such as state provider taxes and directed payment programs. The Trump Administration has also been imposing stricter requirements for people to enroll and retain health insurance under the Affordable Care Act ('ACA') exchanges. And at year end, the federal subsidies that help people pay for plans on the ACA exchanges are set to expire. If Congress doesn’t extend these subsidies, another 5.1 million people could lose insurance coverage, according to the CBO. As a result of all these changes, hospitals could face reduced Medicaid payments and an increase in uncompensated care costs, and managed care companies could face fewer enrollees and adverse selection as healthier members drop coverage.

Given this backdrop, why are we optimistic about the long-term outlook for Health Care?

We think the long-term secular growth drivers for health care are intact. The U.S. population is aging, driven by the aging of the baby boomer population and increased longevity, fueled by new ways to diagnose, monitor, and treat diseases. In 2022, there were 57.8 million Americans aged 65 and older, representing 17.3% of the population. The Census Bureau estimates older Americans will represent over 20% of the population in 2030. Health care spending is expected to grow faster than GDP and reach 20.3% of GDP by 2033 (note: this projection occurred prior to passage of the OBBBA). Innovation in the sector is robust. Despite recent underperformance, we remain encouraged that Health Care often outperforms the broad market over the long term. As indicated in the chart below, the Russell 3000 Health Care Index outperformed the Russell 3000 Index 65% of the time on a rolling 5-year basis and 76% of the time over rolling 10-year periods, meaning the longer one invests in Health Care, the more likely the sector is to outperform the broad market.

Health Care Outperformance vs. Broader Market Percentage of time Health Care sector outperformed Broad Based Benchmarks over different time periods from inception of Russell 3000 Health Care Index (7/1/1996) through 6/30/2025

Rolling Return Period

Performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. The indexes are unmanaged. Index performance is not Fund performance. Investors cannot invest directly in an index.

Once there is greater clarity around policy changes, investors can reassess the earnings impact and value companies under the new rules. With the OBBBA now signed into law and the Trump Administration‘s announcements about tariffs and MFN drug pricing likely to come in the near term, we think policy changes are largely priced in, and there is potential for some of the draconian changes to be modified in the future, given likely political pushback. Polls show that the OBBBA’s health care cuts are unpopular with the public. With respect to the FDA personnel changes, so far the FDA appears to be functioning and drugs are getting approved. Leaders at the FDA have made encouraging public statements about streamlining the drug approval process. In fact, the OBBBA expands the number of orphan drugs that are exempted from Medicare price negotiations, which should promote more innovation in the biopharmaceutical industry, and by extension, bolster life science tools suppliers. As to academic research, Congress decides the NIH budget and Congress has been supportive of annual increases. A federal judge ordered the NIH to reinstate 800 research grants that have been canceled, and another judge issued an injunction blocking the NIH from implementing the cap on indirect costs. Regarding Medicaid cuts, there has been speculation that blue states will try to maintain enrollment with work requirement workarounds, and because most of the major changes do not come into effect until 2027 and 2028, hospitals have time to lobby lawmakers and build public support to prevent the cuts from ultimately going into effect.

We think the companies in which the Fund invests are unique, competitively advantaged growth companies with innovative products that can improve patient outcomes and/or lower health care costs. Eli Lilly and Company’s (LLY) new GLP-1 medicines offer a broad range of health benefits, including blood sugar control and weight loss, reduced risk of heart attack, stroke and heart failure, improved kidney function, reduction in sleep apnea, and could potentially reduce substance abuse disorders and treat neurocognitive illnesses like Alzheimer’s. Argenx SE’s (ARGX, OTCPK:ARGNF) Vyvgart is transforming the treatment of various rare autoimmune diseases like myasthenia gravis and chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy. Boston Scientific Corporation’s (BSX) Farapulse pulsed field ablation system is transforming the treatment of atrial fibrillation through the use of targeted electrical pulses that make procedures more precise, faster, and safer. Intuitive Surgical, Inc.’s (ISRG) robotic surgical systems are making surgical procedures less invasive, which reduces pain, results in less blood loss, and allows for faster recovery for the patient, while surgeons benefit from enhanced precision, dexterity, and visualization during the procedure. Insmed Incorporated’s (INSM) innovative new medicines for respiratory diseases have the potential to be a game changer for patients. RadNet, Inc. (RDNT) is using AI algorithms to enhance the accuracy of breast cancer screening mammograms, leading to earlier cancer detection and reducing the number of unnecessary patient call backs.

These are just a few examples of why we are optimistic about the long-term outlook for Health Care and the Fund. We continue to follow our process for identifying investment opportunities and creating a portfolio of competitively advantaged growth companies with strong management teams.

The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate; an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. The Russell 3000® Index measures the performance of the largest 3,000 U.S. companies representing approximately 98% of the investable U.S. equity market, as of the most recent reconstitution. The Fund invests in small and medium sized companies whose securities may be thinly traded and more difficult to sell during market downturns.

data.title: Puma Biotechnology, Inc. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Puma Biotechnology, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Centene Corporation: The Risks Outweigh The Potential Returns
data.description:
Thesis

The risks of investing in shares of Centene Corporation (NYSE:CNC) outweigh the potential returns due to its ostensibly strong but actually weak balance sheet and the unfavorable macro- political, economic, and regulatory environments that it is operating under that promise to materially reduce its revenues and profits in the foreseeable future. Therefore, I would counsel caution for anyone who is enticed to buy its stock due to its dramatic price decline. Since the stock price still does not provide any meaningful margin of safety that is any excess of the fair market value of its business in the face of deteriorating fundamentals as explained in this article, I rate the stock of CNC a Sell.

Business Overview

The Company specializes in managing government-sponsored health insurance programs such as Medicaid, Medicare, and Marketplace plans, and also offers some commercial insurance products. While it also provides related healthcare services like pharmacy benefit management and behavioral health, its core business is health insurance—particularly for low-income, underinsured, or uninsured populations through government contracts.

Recent Development

On July 1, 2025, Centene submitted an Form 8-K to the SEC stating that, based on information provided by an independent actuarial firm, Wakely, covering 22 of Centene's 29 Marketplace states—and representing approximately 72% of the Company's Marketplace membership—the Company needed to reduce its 2025 EPS projection by $2.75 from its prior estimate of $7.25. The Company made such an adjustment upon discovery that "market growth" is lower, but "market morbidity" is higher than expected, which in plain English means the Company is not growing its business there as much as expected but is making more claim payments than expected.

Assuming the Company would need to make a similar downward adjustment for the remaining 28% of its Marketplace membership, the EPS should be cut by about $3.82 in total, i.e., an additional $1.07 for those 28%, resulting in a projected 2025 EPS of $3.43.

On July 25, the Company announced through the quarterly earnings call that its new projection for adjusted earnings per share in 2025 was approximately $1.75—instead of its previous projection of at least $7.25—after reporting a loss of about $0.50 per share for the latest quarter. During the call, the Company cited six reasons for such adjustment, including a $4.55 billion pretax earnings reduction resulted from the Marketplace morbidity trend (meaning, in plain English, that enrollees are sicker than projected) and a $2.1 billion pretax earnings resulted from a 93.5 Medicaid Health Benefit Ratio (indicating that 93.5% of premium revenues will be paid out for the medical care of the enrollees).

This July 1 disclosure triggered an immediate stock price drop from about $55 to about $33. The stock has declined further since then. It is currently traded at around $25.

Book Value In Perspective

The Book Value of the company’s total stockholders' equity is misleadingly high. Though it's accurate in terms of accounting standards, it gives a rosier picture than warranted. With a $3.5 billion "real" book value (as I've calculated below with the exclusion of goodwill), the fact that the Company reports a $26.5 billion book value relative to a $13 billion market capitalization is no longer a sound reason to buy the stock. My price to book ratio is around 4, not the apparently enticing 0.5.

The bullish argument that the company overall is undervalued relative to its book value is flatly wrong. At first glance at the latest Form 10-K of the Company filed with the SEC for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024, one sees total stockholders’ equity listed at $26.5 billion near the end of the consolidated balance sheet. This is approximately twice the size of the Company’s market cap as of today.

But does this really provide a current buyer of the stock with as much margin of safety as it appears? Probably not. The reasons can be found by closely reviewing the same consolidated balance sheet:

The Company’s assets include goodwill of more than $17.6 billion. Practically speaking, when a company pays over the book value of any asset acquired whether via an asset purchase or a stock deal, the excess amount should not be baked into the buyer's equity book value, or at least not into the fair market value of such equity.

However, for accounting purposes and to balance the books, such excess must be recorded on the buyer’s balance sheet first as part of the value of the acquired assets and then flow down to the stockholders' equity. This inflates the reported equity book value, regardless of whether the acquisition premium was justified.

In any event, one cannot assume that, in a liquidation scenario, the $17.6 billion in goodwill could be realized at all. It also goes without saying that in the event of liquidity challenges, this amount cannot be deployed by the Company to repay loans or to cover the Company’s medical costs or selling, general, or administrative expenses.

In short, deducting this $17.6 billion from the total assets—and thus from the stockholders’ equity—yields a “real” or "practical" book value of about $9 billion.

Just one line down on the same balance sheet is another $5.5 billion in intangible assets primarily consisting of tradenames, purchased contract rights and customer relationships and internally developed systems and procedures. Again, in a liquidation scenario, one cannot assume that this $5.5 billion could be realized. Like goodwill, these assets cannot be used to repay debt or fund operations during a liquidity crunch. Deducting this $5.5 billion from the Company’s stockholders’ equity yields a “real” book value of approximately $3.5 billion. Had the $26.5 billion book value been derived more from tangible assets such as cash, securities, real estate, it would provide more meaningful cushion against any future shocks of earnings declines or liquidity challenges.

Of course, the above represents a pessimistic outlook, assigning zero value to goodwill and intangible assets. However, in the face of revenue declines and earnings shortfalls, companies often must write down such assets, further reducing GAAP EPS, credit ratings, and stock prices. This pessimistic view is not unjustified, because at the end of the day, assets like goodwill cannot be monetized therefore deployed to repay debts or fund operations. They are on the balance sheet of the Company primarily to reflect the premiums paid for certain assets of the Company.

The Company’s Top and Bottom Lines

Centene's low stock price is justified by the challenges imposed upon the top and bottom lines of the Company by the macro-economic and regulatory environments.

The bullish argument here is that the downward earnings trend is temporary—or, at the very least, that the stock price decline has been disproportionate to the expected decline in earnings. In other words, Mr. Market may have punished Centene’s stock more harshly than what the deteriorations of its fundamentals warrant.

However, such optimism can hardly withstand the scrutiny of key financial metrics on the company's profitability and revenue growth, nor can it be justified by the overall unfavorable regulatory and political environment in which the Company operates.

Let's start with the challenges on its top line.

According to KFF, enrollment in the ACA Marketplace hovered between 11.7 million and 12.7 million from 2015 to 2020. Starting in 2021, however, enrollment began to climb steadily—from approximately 12 million to 24.3 million by 2025. This dramatic increase over the past five years was driven by favorable policy changes, pandemic-related fiscal stimulus, and enhanced outreach and user experience—all of which now appear to be reversing.

According to this KFF article, the non-partisan Congressional Budget Office (CBO) projects $793 billion in Medicaid-related federal spending cuts over 2025–2034, leading to approximately 10.3 million fewer Medicaid enrollees by 2034 thanks to the big elephant in the room, that is, the One Big Beautiful Bill. The CBO also projects that these Medicaid cuts are expected to result in 7.8 million additional uninsured Americans by 2034, and across all healthcare provisions—including Medicaid changes and ACA marketplace alterations—the uninsured population is expected to rise by 16 million by 2034.

In addition to the headwinds of the One Big Beautiful Bill at the federal level, the Company also faces the risks at the state levels. If the ten or so states that have not adopted the Medicaid expansion under the ACA continue to reject expansion (especially after One Big Beautiful Bill repeals ACA incentives), millions of potential enrollees will remain in the “coverage gap”—earning too much for traditional Medicaid but too little for ACA subsidies, making them less likely to enroll in Obamacare, especially if the unemployment and inflation in the country move into the wrong directions.

Looking at the chart and the above CBO’s long-term projections, it is difficult to conclude that the headwinds facing the Company are merely short-term or non-structural. Management is confronted with the daunting task of pivoting quickly and effectively in response to the impending dramatic declines in revenue caused by such structural changes.

Now let's move through its income statement to reach the bottom line.

The Company already operates on a thin margin—one that is becoming even thinner—leaving it with very limited room to maneuver through financial turbulence as the tide turns. According to its most recent Form 10-K, the company had to pay out 88.3% of its revenue—primarily consisting of premiums received—for the medical costs of its enrollees. This figure, known as the HBR (Healthcare Benefit Ratio), reflects how much of each dollar earned is spent directly on care. In addition, its SG&A (Selling, General, and Administrative) expenses accounted for 8.5% of revenue. In the latest Form 10-Q for the quarter ending June 30, 2025, the situation has worsened: the HBR increased to 93%, up from 87.6% in the same quarter of 2024.

These pressures are exacerbated by the likelihood of a dramatic decline in enrollment. If the enrollment gains made over the last five years reverse—which appears highly likely—the number of enrollees may fall from 24.3 million to somewhere in the mid-teens. Such a drop would translate to a revenue reduction of 25% to 40%, assuming no premium increases for the remaining members.

Yes, the Company could attempt to offset this by raising premiums per member. But that would likely trigger further reductions in membership, eroding revenue even more. Worse, the members who remain despite the withdrawal of subsidies are likely to require more long-term and expensive care, further compressing profit margins through a deteriorating HBR. While the Company may also attempt to reduce SG&A costs, whether it can do so swiftly and effectively—without impairing operational capacity—remains uncertain.

On balance, any measures the Company might take to counter these headwinds will likely have limited effect. While incremental gains in revenue per enrollee or slight reductions in SG&A might be achieved, they are unlikely to compensate meaningfully for the loss in overall revenue. Under such conditions, increasing EPS from the projected $1.75 would be rather challenging.

Indeed, as mentioned above, the Company has already announced a new EPS projection of approximately $1.75 for 2025—before the full impact of recent changes in public policies and laws has even taken effect. Once those effects are fully felt, a material decline in EPS seems inevitable, even if not strictly proportional to the drop in enrollment.

In fact, one could argue that the market is being optimistic by assigning at $25 price to the stock today. At an EPS of $1.75 and a P/E ratio of about 14—slightly below the sector median of 17—Mr. Market appears to assume that EPS will not decline meaningfully below $1.75. But if EPS does decline substantially due to pressure on the top line and the Company’s inability to reduce costs proportionally and promptly, then the current stock price would only be sustainable if the Company’s P/E exceeds the sector median—a highly unlikely scenario.

Valuation

More logically, Mr. Market would push the stock price down to keep its valuation metrics in line with its peers. Worse yet, if the company turns unprofitable, assessing its intrinsic value becomes even more difficult. This possibility is not far-fetched: in the latest Form 10-Q, the Company reported a quarterly loss of $0.50.

Therefore, it is my view that—given the macroeconomic and regulatory headwinds—Centene is likely to have annual EPS between -$2.00 and $2.00 for the foreseeable future. This outlook broadly aligns with the market’s current valuation of approximately $25 per share. I predict that in the next 2 years or so, the stock trades within the bound of $15 to $25.

The final bullish argument I wish to address is that the Company’s stock trades at a very low price-to-sales and EV-to-sales ratio—both around 0.1x—which is not only far below the sector median of approximately 3.4x, but also well below the Company’s own five-year historical average of about 0.3x.

However, at the end of the day, a business exists to generate profit—not just revenue. A company with substantial revenue (and therefore likely large-scale operations) but extremely low price-to-sales and EV-to-sales multiples often reveals a fundamental problem: it is operating on razor-thin margins. Such thin margins may result from poor execution by management or from an inherently inferior business model.

This Company fits squarely into the inference I outlined above. According to its latest Form 10-Q , it operates with a Health Benefits Ratio (HBR) of 93 and an SG&A expense ratio of 7.1, resulting in a combined ratio of 100.1. If margins deteriorate further for the reasons discussed, which is a scenario the Company clearly faces, its extremely low price-to-sales ratio implies that its large sales volume could paradoxically lead to larger losses than a smaller-scale competitor.

In other words, the Company’s sizable revenue base becomes more of a liability than an asset, as it faces mounting challenges on both its top and bottom lines. This scale hampers its agility, making it difficult to pivot effectively when confronted with external headwinds beyond its control—such as policy shifts—thereby compounding its operational difficulties.

Risks to My Views

It’s possible that the political winds may shift after the next midterm or presidential election, restoring more progressive policies that could favor the Company’s enrollees and therefore the Company's top and bottom lines. But such a development is too uncertain to assess or bet on intelligently.

If it were to happen, however, the Company’s revenue could return to levels seen in recent years, and its margins and earnings could expand dramatically from the currently depressed $1.75 projected EPS. In that case, the stock’s P/E and price-to-sales multiples would likely be re-rated upward. Given the Company’s enormous revenue base relative to its current market capitalization, its price-to-revenue ratio—currently under 0.1—appears extremely depressed. If this multiple were to catch up to that of a close peer like Molina Health Inc., which trades at around 0.2, the Company’s market cap could double. If this multiple were to catch up to that of its own last five year average of about 0.3x, the Company’s market cap could triple. The broader health insurance sector trades at a median price-to-revenue ratio above 3, indicating even more potential upside if market and regulatory conditions shift favorably, and/or if the Company substantially improves its execution thereby reducing its combined ratio.

This is plausible because the Company operates with a very large revenue base but razor-thin margins. If margins improve due to external conditions—without any operational changes -- coupled with revenue growth—its market cap could more than triple without the stock appearing expensive relative to revenues.

The Verdict

That said, speculating on the direction of national politics is closer to gambling than investing. Even sophisticated investors who understand Centene’s business well and can thoughtfully assess the impact of policies like the One Big and Beautiful Bill may not realize alpha unless the macro-regulatory environment shifts decisively in the Company’s favor in the foreseeable future.

For the average retail investor—who may not fully grasp these complex, evolving dynamics—it is likely more prudent to stay on the sidelines. Missing a potential opportunity is a far smaller risk than betting on a dramatic turnaround of the Company, especially given the Company’s inherently inferior business model and the unfavorable policy environment it faces.

data.title: Giverny Trims Progressive, Exits M&T, Adds Kinsale And Fiserv
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Giverny Capital Asset Management Q2 2025 Letter.

We had a handful of transactions during the quarter.

We exited M&T Bank (MTB), a Buffalo-based regional bank with a strong balance sheet and a conservative management team, trading at a modest price. This is not the kind of business I like to sell, but the longer I owned M&T, the more concerned I grew about its growth prospects.

M&T competes in mostly slower growing US markets, including Northeastern cities like Buffalo, Rochester, Hartford and Baltimore. With bank deposit market share as high as 60% in Buffalo, it is unlikely that another bank would try to open branches to compete with M&T. In turn, this means M&T has deep relationships with many of the best clients in town.

The fact is, however, that these markets barely grow. M&T’s organic loan growth over the past decade was about 1%. Worse, the rise of non-bank lenders (also known as private credit) means customers have more options for loans than they used to. A growing business in Buffalo no longer needs a local bank relationship to get a loan. Parallel to this, digital banks like Capital One (COF) now pay up to double the interest on deposits as branchbased banks, and brokers like Charles Schwab offer banking services, including loans, as part of a brokerage account relationship.

I viewed sluggish growth as acceptable given the less competitive nature of M&T’s core markets. However, during the first quarter earnings call and in a subsequent meeting with management, M&T called out increasing competition from private credit. M&T grew earnings at a modest 7% clip over the past decade, one in which all banks benefited from massive stimulus that inflated bank deposits and made it easier for consumers and small businesses to pay back loans. The decade ahead seems like it could be more competitive, both in terms of adding deposits as well as making and collecting loans.

The bank’s PE multiple is low, and it is so well-capitalized that it could generate a decent return for owners by making whatever sensible loans it could and using excess capital to buy back stock. M&T could also be a very attractive candidate for purchase by a fastergrowing bank that could better deploy its excess capital. There are paths to success, but ultimately, I felt we could find better growth elsewhere. M&T stock has risen since our sale.

We trimmed our large holding in Progressive Corp. (PGR) during the quarter at roughly $282. We also sold some shares in the first quarter, such that through June 30th we sold 29% of the holding in the model portfolio. Clients who have been with us for any length of time will have significant capital gains, as Progressive stock doubled in the past two years.

Selling winners because they’ve exceeded your price target generally does not work. The best companies tend to outperform expectations over time and when you own a business that does this, the smarter strategy is to rethink your expectations rather than take profits. We still own a substantial weight in Progressive, and I continue to believe it is among the best managed companies in our portfolio. But auto insurance has a cycle, and right now we appear to be at the top of the cycle: rates are at historically high levels, industry profitability is rising rapidly (which could catch the eyes of regulators and create downward future pricing pressure), and rivals are ramping up their advertising to try to grow faster. Meanwhile, Progressive is earning record earnings and trading at high multiples of those earnings, while also growing far faster than the industry.

Barring a major surprise over the next six months, Progressive will double its revenue over the five years ending December 2025 and nearly double its net income. Competitor Allstate (ALL), by comparison, will grow revenue about half as fast as Progressive over the fiveyear period, and likely will earn less money in 2025 than it did five years ago. Progressive has managed through this strange post-pandemic period far better than its leading rivals and is well-positioned for the future.

I suspect, however, that we are entering a period in which rate competition will lead to weaker profitability for all players. I feel Progressive’s future return is likely to be lower than the past five years. So, I’m comfortable holding it at a smaller size. As car insurance has cyclical characteristics, I remain open to increasing the position in the future.

In terms of additions, we purchased more shares of our insurer Kinsale Corp (KNSL) during the quarter. Mr. Market is a volatile fellow and periodically he does weird things, such as sell great companies because they miss short-term growth targets. He did this in April when Kinsale reported slightly disappointing revenue growth and losses related to Southern California wildfires. We added to our position in late April at $419. Kinsale continues to have the lowest expense structure of its peer group, and among the highest growth rates and profit margins. Insurance is a commodity business, so players like Progressive in car insurance and Kinsale in commercial property and casualty are going to win over time by being efficient. Kinsale stock finished the quarter at $480.

We added to Fiserv (FI) during the quarter. In May, Fiserv reported slower growth for its Clover payment processing business – Clover is one of several portable terminals that you may have seen restaurants use to process credit card payments. When Clover grew 8% during the first quarter rather than the expected 10%, the stock dropped by 30%. Again, Mr. Market gets hyper sometimes.

Fiserv is one of the most durable growth stories of the past four decades, having grown earnings by at least 10% for 39 straight years. It acts as the primary technology provider to several thousand regional banks. In turn, the banks actively sell Fiserv products like Clover to their customers, allowing them to transmit credit card payments directly to their bank accounts with a full accounting of the day’s receipts. Clover itself is only about 15% of Fiserv’s total business – the company has a menu of other products that its bank partners sell to their own customers – but it has great potential.

Even with temporarily slower growth from Clover, Fiserv should grow earnings about 15% this year, to roughly $10 per share. Wall Street analyst consensus calls for EPS of nearly $12 in 2026. We added to the position at about $169 in May and it closed the quarter a bit higher than that. The current price is just over 14x next year’s earnings estimate in a market that trades for more than 20x forward estimates. There are certainly competing payment terminals that restaurants and small retailers can buy, but none have a massive installed base of regional banks that actively sell and support the product.

Disclosures
